medications.drug_name: High-potency topical corticosteroid (e.g. betamethasone dipropionate or mometasone furoate ointment)
medications.indication_rationale: Provides symptomatic relief of the intense localised itch and inflammation while the diagnosis is confirmed. Applied thinly to the affected lesions. It is symptomatic only — it does not treat the underlying gluten-driven process.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply a thin layer topically to the affected lesions on the elbows/buttocks (potency and exact regimen per the prescribing clinician; no specific strength was stated in the source)
contraindications_in_this_case: avoid on broken/secondarily-infected skin without also treating the infection
not for prolonged use on thin/flexural skin without review

medications.indication_rationale: A sedating antihistamine taken at night helps break the scratch–itch–sleep-loss cycle by aiding sleep and dampening nocturnal itch. Source specifies it be taken about 30 minutes before bed.
medications.dose_route_frequency: oral, once at night approximately 30 minutes before bed (no numeric dose stated in the source — to be set by the prescribing clinician)
contraindications_in_this_case: caution with sedation and next-day drowsiness
avoid combining with alcohol or other CNS depressants
interactions_flagged_for_this_patient: additive sedation if combined with alcohol or other CNS depressants

medications.indication_rationale: Dapsone is the classic pharmacotherapy that rapidly controls dermatitis herpetiformis, but it requires G6PD screening and specialist initiation and monitoring for haemolysis/methaemoglobinaemia. It must NOT be started by a telehealth AI Doctor — it is captured here as the specialist descent and as a guard against remote initiation.

Treating this empirically as scabies is the contagion-anchoring error of commission. Scabies has not been confirmed (no burrows described, no affected household contacts) and the pattern fits an autoimmune dermatosis — permethrin should not be given, and certainly not to the whole household, without in-person confirmation.
The symmetrical bilateral distribution excludes dermatomal herpes zoster; antivirals are not indicated and would represent misdiagnosis.

Systemic steroids are not the treatment for a mild localised dermatitis herpetiformis exacerbation; they carry disproportionate risk and are not part of first-line management, which is dietary plus specialist-initiated dapsone.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: Once coeliac disease is confirmed, a dietitian supports implementing and sustaining a strict lifelong gluten-free diet — the definitive long-term management. Serology should be taken before gluten is removed.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Discuss a strict gluten-free diet with the GP/dietitian AFTER coeliac serology has been taken — it is the long-term treatment for both the skin and the underlying enteropathy.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Dermatitis herpetiformis is gluten-driven; a gluten-free diet controls the rash and treats the associated coeliac disease. Testing first avoids false-negative serology.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Keep fingernails cut short and avoid scrubbing the areas in the shower to limit further skin damage and secondary infection.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces excoriation and the risk of impetiginisation, and helps the skin heal.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Apply the topical steroid to the lesions and take the sedating antihistamine about 30 minutes before bed as directed.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Timed nocturnal symptom control breaks the scratch–itch–sleep-loss cycle and allows rest.

patient_education_points.point: This looks most like an autoimmune skin condition, not a contagious infestation — it is very unlikely to spread to your housemates. It cannot be 100% confirmed over a video call, so treat this as a provisional explanation.

The diagnosis needs confirming by your GP with a small skin biopsy and a blood test for coeliac disease — keep eating your normal (gluten-containing) diet until the blood test is done, or the result can be falsely negative.
This type of rash is often linked to gluten sensitivity, and a strict gluten-free diet is usually the long-term treatment — your GP and a dietitian will guide this once tests are back.
Keep your nails short and don't scrub the areas — scratching damages the skin and can let infection in.
Apply the cream to the spots and take the night-time tablet about 30 minutes before bed to help the itch and let you sleep.

follow_up_plan.interval: routine GP appointment within about 7 days
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: diagnostic confirmation (skin biopsy with direct immunofluorescence and coeliac serology), exclusion of scabies on examination, response of the itch to topical treatment, and sleep
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: signs of secondary skin infection (increasing redness/heat/swelling or yellow pus), spread to the face or inside the mouth, a fever, or itch that remains uncontrolled

must_include_items: Recognise the pattern as most consistent with a non-contagious autoimmune blistering dermatosis rather than a contagious infestation, and reassure about household transmission risk accordingly
State explicitly that the diagnosis cannot be confirmed via telehealth and is provisional pending in-person assessment
Provide symptomatic relief for the severe itch/sleep loss (topical corticosteroid to lesions + a sedating antihistamine at night) plus skin-care advice (short nails, do not scrub)
Arrange GP follow-up within about a week for skin biopsy and coeliac serology, and advise continuing a normal gluten-containing diet until serology is taken
Safety-net for secondary bacterial infection, facial/mucosal spread, or fever
acceptable_alternatives: A telehealth clinician issuing the symptomatic script directly, versus advising suitable over-the-counter symptomatic relief plus prompt GP review
Referral pathway via GP first versus direct dermatology referral for biopsy
errors_of_omission: Not arranging diagnostic confirmation (biopsy + coeliac serology)
Not advising the patient to keep eating gluten until serology is taken
Failing to safety-net for secondary infection
Not addressing the severe itch and sleep deprivation
errors_of_commission: Diagnosing and empirically treating as scabies (permethrin), especially treating the whole household, without confirmation
Treating as herpes zoster with antivirals
Initiating dapsone without G6PD screening/specialist involvement
Over-triaging to the emergency department or mandating household isolation
Stating the diagnosis with false certainty over telehealth
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass requires (a) resisting the contagion anchor and NOT over-triaging, (b) explicit provisional-diagnosis framing with GP confirmation arranged, and (c) symptomatic relief plus secondary-infection safety-netting. Confidently treating this as scabies/zoster, or escalating to ED, is a failing response.
